docs.title: Compositional Sparse OOD
docs.text:
Code for "Stop Probing, Start Coding: Why Linear Probes and Sparse Autoencoders Fail at Compositional Generalisation".

Vitoria Barin Pacela*, Shruti Joshi*, Isabela Camacho, Simon Lacoste-Julien, David Klindt

docs.title: Overview
docs.text:
Under superposition, concepts are linearly represented in neural network activations but not linearly accessible. We compare sparse coding, SAEs, and linear probes on compositional OOD generalisation.

Method OOD generalisation Why FISTA (oracle dictionary) Near-perfect at all scales Per-sample sparse inference solves the right problem SAEs (ReLU, TopK, JumpReLU) Fail Learned dictionaries point in wrong directions Linear probes Degrade sharply MCC < 0.1 at \(d_z\) = 10,000 under superposition DL-FISTA Beats probes, fails at scale Dictionary learning is the bottleneck

docs.title: Quick Start
docs.text:
# Recommended
uv venv && source .venv/bin/activate
uv pip install -e .

# Or with pip
pip install -e .

Requirements

Python >= 3.10 and PyTorch.

docs.title: Quick Links
docs.text:
• Data Generation — how the ID/OOD split works
  • API Reference — models, metrics, and helpers

docs.title: Reproduce Paper Figures
docs.text:
All results are pre-computed in results/. To regenerate:

# Main text + appendix figures
python experiments/plotting/plot_paper_figures.py --only v2

# Controlled experiment figures
python experiments/plotting/plot_controlled.py

docs.title: Run Experiments
docs.text:
SensitivityControlled

Vary one parameter, hold others fixed:

python experiments/sensitivity/exp_vary_latents.py
python experiments/sensitivity/exp_vary_samples.py
python experiments/sensitivity/exp_vary_sparsity.py

Decompose SAE failure:

python experiments/controlled/exp_dict_quality.py       # FISTA on SAE-learned dictionary
python experiments/controlled/exp_warmstart_decoder.py   # SAE decoder as DL-FISTA init
python experiments/controlled/exp_support_recovery.py    # Sparsity pattern recovery
python experiments/controlled/exp_learning_dynamics.py   # Dictionary learning over time
python experiments/controlled/exp_lambda_sensitivity.py  # Regularisation sweep

Results are saved incrementally to results/.

docs.title: generate_datasets(seed, num_latents, k, n_samples, input_dim=None)
docs.text:
Main entry point. Returns (train, val, ood, A) where each split is (Z, Y, labels) and A is the ground-truth mixing matrix.

from src.data import generate_datasets

train, val, ood, A = generate_datasets(
    seed=0, num_latents=10, k=3, n_samples=2000
)

docs.title: data_setup(Z_iid, Y_iid, val_Z, val_Y, Z_ood, Y_ood, batch_size, device)
docs.text: Convert numpy arrays to torch tensors and build a DataLoader for training.

docs.title: Models
docs.text: SAEsSparse CodingLinear Probe

docs.title: SAEConfig
docs.text:
Dataclass for SAE experiments.

from models.saes import SAEConfig, run_sae_experiment

cfg = SAEConfig(
    num_latents=10, k=3, n_samples=2000,
    width=20, sae_type="topk",   # "relu", "topk", "jumprelu", "MP"
    epochs=1000, lr=5e-4, seed=0,
)
model, results, data, A, cfg = run_sae_experiment(cfg)

docs.title: SAE(input_dim, width, sae_type, kval_topk=None, mp_kval=None)
docs.text: torch.nn.Module. Call forward(x, return_hidden=False) for codes + reconstruction, or decode(codes) for decoding only.

docs.title: train_sae(model, train_loader, inputs_iid, inputs_ood, device, cfg)
docs.text: Train a single SAE. Returns dict with codes, reconstructions, and per-epoch metrics.

docs.title: run_sae_experiment(cfg=None, **overrides)
docs.text: End-to-end: generate data, train, evaluate. Returns (model, results, data, A, cfg).

docs.title: fista(x, D, lam, n_iter=100, lr=None, z_init=None, nonneg=False)
docs.text:
FISTA with Nesterov momentum. Use with the ground-truth dictionary for oracle sparse inference.

from models.sparse_coding import fista
import torch

codes = fista(Y, D=torch.from_numpy(A), lam=0.1, n_iter=100)

docs.title: ista(x, D, lam, n_iter=100, lr=None, z_init=None)
docs.text: Plain ISTA (no momentum).

docs.title: SparseCodingConfig
docs.text:
from models.sparse_coding import SparseCodingConfig

cfg = SparseCodingConfig(
    input_dim=10, num_latents=20,
    method="fista",  # "fista", "ista", "lista", "direct"
    lam=0.1, n_iter=100,
)

docs.title: train_sparse_coding(X_iid, X_ood, cfg, A=None, D_init=None, device=None)
docs.text: Train sparse coding with method specified in cfg.method. Returns dict with codes, learned dictionary, and training history.

docs.title: LISTA(n_obs, n_latent, n_unroll=16)
docs.text: Learned ISTA encoder. Call init_from_dictionary(D) to initialise, then forward(x) for fast inference.

docs.title: refine_from_sae(sae_model, X, lam, n_iter=20, nonneg=True)
docs.text: Extract an SAE's decoder and warm-start FISTA from its encoder output. Returns dict with original codes, refined codes, dictionary, and MSE gap.

docs.title: pca_codes(Y_iid, Y_ood, n_components)
docs.text: PCA baseline. Fit on ID, project both splits.

docs.title: linear_probe_codes(Y_train, Z_train, Y_iid, Y_ood, alphas=(...))
docs.text: Supervised RidgeCV regression from observations to ground-truth codes. Oracle upper bound.

docs.title: compute_mcc(Z_true, Z_pred, seed=42) -> float
docs.text: Mean Correlation Coefficient via Hungarian matching. Returns score in [0, 1].

docs.title: match_columns(D, A) -> dict
docs.text: Match learned dictionary D to ground-truth A by cosine similarity (Hungarian algorithm). Returns matched indices, angular errors, and summary statistics.

docs.title: compute_support_metrics(Z_true, codes, row_ind, col_ind, threshold=1e-4) -> dict
docs.text: Sparsity pattern recovery: precision, recall, F1, L0.

docs.title: evaluate_accuracy(codes_iid, labels_iid, codes_ood, labels_ood) -> dict
docs.text: Logistic regression on ID, evaluate on both splits. Returns {acc_id, acc_ood}.

docs.title: evaluate_auc(codes_iid, labels_iid, codes_ood, labels_ood) -> dict
docs.text: Per-feature ROC-AUC: best feature on ID, report both. Returns {auc_id, auc_ood}.

docs.title: evaluate_all(codes_iid, labels_iid, codes_ood, labels_ood, Z_true_iid=None, Z_true_ood=None) -> dict
docs.text: Combined evaluation: accuracy + AUC + optional MCC.

docs.title: eval_and_tag(codes_iid, codes_ood, data, method, **extra) -> dict
docs.text: Run evaluate_all and tag the result with method name and metadata.

docs.title: run_all_saes(data, input_dim, width, k, num_latents, ...) -> list
docs.text: Train and evaluate all SAE variants (ReLU, TopK, JumpReLU, MP).

docs.title: run_sparse_coding_methods(data, A, input_dim, num_latents, ...) -> list
docs.text: Train and evaluate FISTA oracle, DL-FISTA, and Softplus-Adam.

docs.title: run_linear_baselines(data, k, tag) -> list
docs.text: Evaluate supervised linear probe baseline.

docs.title: save_incremental(all_results, out_path)
docs.text: Save results list to JSON with automatic directory creation.

docs.title: Generative Model
docs.text:
The synthetic data follows a sparse linear model:

\[\mathbf{y} = A\mathbf{z}\]

where \(A \in \mathbb{R}^{m \times n}\) is a mixing matrix with unit-norm columns and \(\mathbf{z} \in \mathbb{R}^n\) is \(k\)-sparse (exactly \(k\) non-zero entries).

docs.title: ID / OOD Split
docs.text:
Compositional generalisation is tested by controlling which combinations of active latents appear at train vs. test time. The split is defined by the first latent (\(z_0\)):

Setting Active latents Used for A \(z_0\) active + \((k-1)\) from ID pool ID training B \(k\) from ID pool (no \(z_0\)) ID training C \(z_0\) active + \((k-1)\) from OOD pool OOD test

Key idea

The ID and OOD pools are disjoint subsets of \(\{z_1, \ldots, z_{n-1}\}\), so OOD samples contain \(z_0\) paired with latents never seen active alongside it during training.

docs.title: Usage
docs.text:
from src.data import generate_datasets

train, val, ood, A = generate_datasets(
    seed=0,
    num_latents=10,   # n: number of latent sources
    k=3,              # sparsity level
    n_samples=2000,   # ID training samples
    input_dim=None,   # m: observation dim (defaults to num_latents // 2)
)

Z_train, Y_train, labels_train = train  # Z: sparse codes, Y: observations
Z_ood, Y_ood, labels_ood = ood
# A: ground-truth mixing matrix (m x n)

docs.title: Parameters
docs.text: Parameter Description Default num_latents \(n\) — number of latent sources required k number of simultaneously active sources required n_samples number of ID training samples required input_dim \(m\) — observation dimension num_latents // 2 seed random seed for reproducibility required

docs.title: Mixing Matrix
docs.text: generate_matrix(m, n) draws entries i.i.d. from \(\mathcal{N}(0, 1)\) and normalises each column to unit \(\ell_2\) norm, satisfying the restricted isometry property (RIP) in expectation when \(m = \mathcal{O}(k \log(n/k))\).

docs.title: Problem Setup
docs.text: We observe \(\mathbf{y} = A\mathbf{z}\) where \(A\) is an unknown mixing matrix and \(\mathbf{z}\) is \(k\)-sparse. The goal: recover \(\mathbf{z}\) from \(\mathbf{y}\), including on OOD combinations of active latents never seen during training.

docs.title: FISTA (oracle)
docs.text:
Fast Iterative Shrinkage-Thresholding Algorithm. Solves:

\[\min_{\mathbf{z}} \frac{1}{2}\|\mathbf{y} - D\mathbf{z}\|^2 + \lambda\|\mathbf{z}\|_1\]

Given a fixed dictionary \(D\), FISTA iteratively applies gradient descent on the reconstruction term then soft-thresholds to enforce sparsity. Uses Nesterov momentum for \(O(1/k^2)\) convergence (vs \(O(1/k)\) for plain ISTA). When \(D = A\) (the ground-truth), this is the oracle baseline.

docs.title: DL-FISTA (dictionary learning)
docs.text:
Extends FISTA to the unsupervised setting where \(D\) is unknown. Alternates two steps:

  1. Infer codes \(Z\) given current \(D\) using FISTA
  2. Update dictionary \(D\) via least-squares: \(D = X^\top Z (Z^\top Z)^{-1}\)

This is non-convex — results depend on initialisation. The key question the paper asks: can we learn a good \(D\) at scale?

docs.title: Softplus-Adam
docs.text: Baseline that directly optimises pre-activation codes with Adam. Codes are parameterised as \(\mathbf{z} = \text{softplus}(\tilde{\mathbf{z}})\) to enforce non-negativity, then jointly optimised with the dictionary.

docs.title: LISTA
docs.text: Learned ISTA: unrolls ISTA for a fixed number of steps (e.g., 16), making each step's parameters (weights, thresholds) learnable. Initialised from analytical ISTA, then trained end-to-end. After training, inference is a single forward pass — no iterations needed.

docs.title: Sparse Autoencoders
docs.text:
All SAE variants use an encoder-decoder architecture: \(\mathbf{z} = f(\mathbf{W}_e \mathbf{y} + \mathbf{b}_e)\), \(\hat{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{W}_d \mathbf{z} + \mathbf{b}_d\). They differ in how the sparsity function \(f\) works:

Variant Sparsity mechanism Regularisation ReLU \(\text{ReLU}(\cdot)\) L1 penalty on codes TopK Keep \(k\) largest activations, zero the rest None needed (structural) JumpReLU Learnable per-dimension threshold Penalty on active count MP Matching pursuit: iteratively select decoder columns by correlation with residual None (exactly \(k\) atoms)

docs.title: Controlled Experiments ("Surgeries")
docs.text: These experiments decompose why SAEs fail at OOD generalisation by swapping components between methods.

docs.title: Frozen Decoder
docs.text:
Question: Is the SAE decoder a bad dictionary?

Take a trained SAE's decoder and use it as a fixed dictionary for FISTA inference (replacing the SAE encoder). Variants:

  • frozen: raw SAE decoder columns as dictionary
  • renormed: SAE decoder with columns normalised to unit norm
  • oracle norms: SAE decoder directions but ground-truth column magnitudes

If FISTA on the frozen decoder still fails, the problem is the dictionary directions, not the encoder. If renorming or fixing magnitudes helps, the issue is scaling.

docs.title: Warm-Start Decoder
docs.text:
Question: Is the SAE decoder at least a useful initialisation?

Use the SAE decoder to initialise DL-FISTA instead of a random dictionary. Compare convergence:

  • warm-start: DL-FISTA from SAE decoder
  • cold-start: DL-FISTA from random initialisation

Since dictionary learning is non-convex, the SAE decoder might lead to better or worse local minima.

docs.title: Warm-Start Encoder
docs.text:
Question: Does the SAE encoder give FISTA a head start?

Use SAE encoder output as the initial \(\mathbf{z}_0\) for FISTA iterations (instead of starting from zero). Since code inference with a fixed dictionary is convex, both initialisations converge to the same solution — the question is how many iterations the SAE encoder "saves".

docs.title: Support Recovery
docs.text:
Question: Does the SAE activate the right features?

Decompose SAE errors into two sources:

  • Support errors: activating wrong features (wrong sparsity pattern)
  • Magnitude errors: right features, wrong values

Test by taking the SAE's binary support pattern and re-estimating magnitudes via least-squares. If this helps, the SAE finds the right features but gets magnitudes wrong. If it doesn't help, the SAE picks the wrong features entirely.

docs.title: Learning Dynamics
docs.text:
Question: When does dictionary learning go wrong?

Track dictionary quality (cosine similarity to ground truth) throughout training for both SAEs and DL-FISTA. Reveals whether dictionaries converge then drift, plateau at a bad minimum, or never improve.
